title: Sempervivum bethencourtianum
intro: Sempervivum bethencourtianum, also known as the Madeiran houseleek, is a succulent plant species native to the island of Madeira, Portugal. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing pain, and combating infections. The bioactive constituents of Sempervivum bethencourtianum include sesquiterpenoids and flavonoids, which have been shown to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and immunomodulatory activities. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations such as tinctures, infusions, and creams, often in traditional medicine and as a complementary therapy for various health conditions, including skin issues and minor injuries.
taxonomy: Sempervivum bethencourtianum is a species of succulent plant in the family Crassulaceae. It is a member of the Sempervivum genus, which is a group of rosette-forming succulents. This genus is further classified within the tribe Semperviveae, which includes plants with similar characteristics. The Crassulaceae family is a large and diverse group of plants that are primarily found in temperate and subtropical regions. Sempervivum bethencourtianum is native to the Canary Islands, where it has adapted to the local climate and soil conditions.

Additionally, Sempervivum bethencourtianum has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including skin conditions, wounds, and digestive problems.
